title: Today's AI Brief: OpenAI and Anthropic May Be Rivals, but Investors Aren’t Picking Sides
summary: A look at the AI world: OpenAI and Anthropic's investors stay neutral, a White House AI advisor departs, and the AI landscape.
imageAlt: Image from source article for OpenAI and Anthropic May Be Rivals, but Investors Aren’t Picking Sides
imageCaption: OpenAI and Anthropic May Be Rivals, but Investors Aren’t Picking Sides
imageSourceTitle: OpenAI and Anthropic May Be Rivals, but Investors Aren’t Picking Sides

body.text: I'm taking a look at the AI world, and it's clear that things are heating up. Two major players, OpenAI and Anthropic, have been vying for attention, workers, and customers. But surprisingly, their investors aren't picking sides.
OpenAI and Anthropic: A Tale of Two Rivals

According to a report from Wired, OpenAI and Anthropic have been on opposite sides of policy proposals, but their investors seem to be staying neutral. This is interesting because it suggests that investors see value in both companies, regardless of their competitive dynamic. It's like they're hedging their bets, saying, 'Hey, we like both of these horses in the AI race.'

In other news, Sriram Krishnan is leaving his role as White House AI advisor at the end of June. A former tech executive and VC, Krishnan has had a privilege to serve the American people. His departure marks a shift in the White House's AI strategy, but it's unclear what this means for the future of AI policy.

As I look at these headlines, I'm struck by the complexity of the AI landscape. On one hand, you have OpenAI and Anthropic competing for dominance, but their investors staying neutral. On the other hand, you have a key player like Krishnan leaving the White House AI advisory role. It's a reminder that the AI world is constantly evolving, and it's going to be interesting to see how things play out.
